Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% For each client, assign exactly one masseuse and one price.
{assign(Client, Masseuse, Price): masseuse(Masseuse), price(Price)}=1 :- clients(Client).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
diplomats("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% For each diplomat D, assign exactly 1 month M and 1 duration T.
{assign(D, M, T): months(M), duration(T)}=1 :- diplomats(D).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").

% For each company, it launches exactly one rocket (from names) in exactly one month.
{launch(Company, Rocket, Month): names(Rocket), months(Month)}=1 :- companies(Company).
```

```asp

towns("Islesboro"; "Long Barn"; "Tarzana"; "Zearing").

% For each witness W, it is associated with exactly one date D and one town T.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee(1; 2; 3; 4).
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("green"; "pink"; "purple"; "red").

% Define the assignment predicate: each employee is assigned to one section and one riders count.
assign(E, S, R) :- employee(E), section(S), riders(R).

% Each employee is assigned to exactly one section and one riders count.
{assign(E, S, R) : section(S), riders(R)} = 1 :- employee(E).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
winners("Daniel"; "Gabriel"; "Roland"; "Vincent").
price(250; 260; 270; 280).
order(3; 4; 7; 8).

% Define the predicate auction(W, P, O) to represent winner W won at price P in order O.
% We generate all possible assignments of winners to prices and orders.
auction(W, P, O) :- winners(W), price(P), order(O).

% Enforce that each winner has exactly one auction (one price and one order).
{auction(W, P, O): price(P), order(O)}=1 :- winners(W).

% The given predicate: ∃P, P': auction(W, P, 7) ∧ auction(Daniel, P', _) ∧ P = P' - 20
% This is a constraint that must be satisfied in some answer set.
% We express it as: there exists a winner W with auction at order 7 and price P,
% and Daniel has an auction at price P' such that P = P' - 20.
:- not (auction(W, P, 7); auction(Daniel, P2, _); P = P2 - 20), winners(W), price(P), price(P2).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
flier("Katie"; "Neal"; "Troy"; "Yolanda").
month(1; 2; 3; 4).
lucky_charm("coin"; "rabbit's foot"; "talisman"; "wishbone").

% Each flier is associated with exactly one month and one lucky charm.
{in_flier_month(F, M): month(M)}=1 :- flier(F).
{in_flier_charm(F, C): lucky_charm(C)}=1 :- flier(F).

% Clue 1: The passenger with the wishbone is either Troy or Neal.
:- in_flier_charm(F, "wishbone"), F != "Troy", F != "Neal".

% Clue 2: The four fliers are exactly: the rabbit's foot holder, the April leaver, Yolanda, and the February leaver.
% This implies that the set { rabbit's foot holder, April leaver, Yolanda, February leaver } is exactly the set of fliers.
% We model this via a constraint that ensures Yolanda is one of the four roles, and the roles are distinct.

% The four roles must be distinct people.
% So: the rabbit's foot holder ≠ April leaver, ≠ February leaver, ≠ Yolanda (unless equal)
% But more precisely: the four entities are the four fliers, so they must be pairwise distinct unless they refer to same person.

% We enforce that the four roles are all distinct fliers.
% But since there are four fliers, and four roles, and each flier has one month and one charm, we can use the following:

% Define a predicate to capture the four roles.
% We use a derived fact: the set of fliers is exactly the set of:
%   - the one with rabbit's foot
%   - the one leaving in April
%   - Yolanda
%   - the one leaving in February

% We express that Yolanda must be one of the four roles.
% But more importantly: the April leaver and the February leaver are different people.

% We enforce that the four roles are distinct by requiring that:
% - The rabbit's foot holder ≠ April leaver
% - The rabbit's foot holder ≠ February leaver
% - The rabbit's foot holder ≠ Yolanda (unless Yolanda has rabbit's foot)
% But we cannot enforce "unless" directly.

% Instead, we use a minimal set of predicates to capture the identity.

% We introduce a predicate to represent the four roles:
% role(F, Type) where Type is one of: "rabbit_foot", "april", "yolanda", "february"

% But we want minimal predicates.

% Alternative: use a constraint that says:
% The four fliers are exactly the ones satisfying:
%   - has_rabbit_foot(F)
%   - leaves_in_month(F, 4)
%   - F = "Yolanda"
%   - leaves_in_month(F, 2)

% So we define:
% has_rabbit_foot(F) :- in_flier_charm(F, "rabbit's foot").
% leaves_in_month(F, M) :- in_flier_month(F, M).

% Then the set {F | has_rabbit_foot(F) ∨ leaves_in_month(F, 4) ∨ F = "Yolanda" ∨ leaves_in_month(F, 2)} must be exactly the set of fliers.

% But we cannot write set equality directly.

% Instead, we use:
% - Every flier must be one of the four roles.
% - The four roles must be distinct.

% So:
% Each flier is either:
%   - the rabbit's foot holder
%   - the April leaver
%   - Yolanda
%   - the February leaver

% So for each flier F:
%   F = "Yolanda" ∨ has_rabbit_foot(F) ∨ leaves_in_month(F, 4) ∨ leaves_in_month(F, 2)

% But we cannot use equality with constants in ASP unless we use a fact.

% Instead, we define:
%   role(F, "yolanda") :- F = "Yolanda".
%   role(F, "rabbit_foot") :- in_flier_charm(F, "rabbit's foot").
%   role(F, "april") :- in_flier_month(F, 4).
%   role(F, "february") :- in_flier_month(F, 2).

% Then we require that the set of fliers is exactly the set of F such that role(F, T) for T in {"yolanda", "rabbit_foot", "april", "february"}.

% But we can enforce that:
% - There are exactly four such F, and they are all distinct.

% But since we have exactly four fliers, we can enforce:
% - The number of fliers that satisfy role(F, T) for any T is exactly 4, and all are distinct.

% However, to minimize predicates, we can use only one predicate to represent the role set.

% But the minimal set of predicates is:
%   - in_flier_month(F, M)
%   - in_flier_charm(F, C)
%   - and then the constraints from clues.

% But we must include the set identity.

% The minimal way is to use:
%   - in_flier_month/2
%   - in_flier_charm/2
%   - and then constraints.

% However, we need to express the set identity.

% We can do it with:
%   - A constraint that says: every flier is one of the four roles.

% So:
%   :- flier(F), not (F = "Yolanda" ; in_flier_charm(F, "rabbit's foot") ; in_flier_month(F, 4) ; in_flier_month(F, 2)).

% But we cannot use "not" with disjunction in that way.

% Instead, we use:
%   :- flier(F), not (F == "Yolanda"), not in_flier_charm(F, "rabbit's foot"), not in_flier_month(F, 4), not in_flier_month(F, 2).

% This says: if F is not Yolanda, and does not have rabbit's foot, and does not leave in April or February, then it's invalid.

% But that's not correct: because the four roles are not mutually exclusive.

% Actually, the clue says: the four fliers are exactly these four: rabbit's foot holder, April leaver, Yolanda, February leaver.

% So: the set of fliers = { rabbit's foot holder, April leaver, Yolanda, February leaver }

% So: every flier must be one of these four, and there are no others.

% So: for every flier F, F must be either:
%   - Yolanda
%   - the one with rabbit's foot
%   - the one leaving in April

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
islands("Fushil"; "Jujihm"; "Nuhirk"; "Verinya").
years(1754; 1761; 1768; 1775).
cultures("Hakili"; "Manika'i"; "Kukani"; "Wainani").

% For each island I, it is discovered in exactly 1 year Y and associated with exactly 1 culture C.
{discover(I, Y, C): years(Y), cultures(C)}=1 :- islands(I).
```

```asp
{launch(C, R, M): rockets(R), months(M)}=1 :- companies(C).

% Each rocket is launched in exactly one month.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
owners("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").

% Define the single predicate to model the matching: winning_dog(Owner, Breed, Year)
% Each owner is matched to exactly one breed and one year, and vice versa.
% This ensures a one-to-one correspondence across all categories.
{winning_dog(O, B, Y) : breeds(B), years(Y)} = 1 :- owners(O).
{winning_dog(O, B, Y) : owners(O), years(Y)} = 1 :- breeds(B).
{winning_dog(O, B, Y) : owners(O), breeds(B)} = 1 :- years(Y).
```
